Era: The Jukebox: Prelude to Class Clown
Name:
I Love High School
(prod. Ben H. Allen III, Mike Caren & Asher Roth)
Notes:
Uses the same instrumental as "I Love College" by Asher Roth as well as samples "10% Dis" by MC Lyte 
Mimicking fellow Pennsylvania-native Asher Roth’s 2009 song, “I Love College,” Mac Miller reminisces on all the great memories he made during his four years at Taylor Allderdice High School in Pittsburgh.

The song uses the same beat and melody as Roth’s and discusses similar themes, such as parties and more generally, life as a student.

At the time, Miller and Roth were often compared to each other, though Miller publicly distinguished himself from Roth in a 2011 interview with XXL.

Name:
5 O'Clock 
(feat. Boaz) (prod. Uncle Ruck)
Notes:
Track 6 on The High Life
Samples "All in the Course of a Day" by The Lost Generation
Following the main theme of this mixtape, “5 O'Clock” is about Mac’s late night experiences as a marijuana dealer. He revealed in past interviews that he actually did sell marijuana, although he was very bad at it.

The song also chronicles Mac’s life as a hungry emcee on the grind, selling weed to pay for studio time and often having all night recording sessions at ID Labs.

Name: Jerome Weinberg Speaks
Notes:
Track 7 on The High Life
“Jerome Weinberg Speaks (Interlude)” is a skit track in which Mac Miller voices himself as well as fictional student Jerome Weinberg: Mac and Jerome are running against each other for class president.

Name:
Class President 
(prod. Uncle Ruck)
Notes:
Track 8 on The High Life
Samples "Two Dope Boyz (In a Cadillac)" by OutKast
“Class President” immediately follows “Jerome Weinberg Speaks”, a skit in which the scene is set for a class-presidential race involving upcoming rapper Mac Miller versus the nerdy Jerome Weinberg (voiced by Mac). After Jerome delivers his very anti-Mac speech, “Class President” is Mac’s rebuttal that you should vote for him.

Name:
Cruise Control
(feat. Wiz Khalifa) (prod. Mac Miller & Drutang)
Notes:
Track 14 on The High Life
By 2009, Mac had put out a couple mixtapes, and he caught everyone’s attention in the city. Wiz was paying attention to him, I was paying attention to him. One night, Wiz, Mac and Benjy [Grinberg] were at the studio and they decided to do a song together. That was really the first time Wiz did a verse for a younger person in Pittsburgh. They bonded and they formed a good, solid relationship, so it was an easy transition for them to tour together.

(prod. B [dot] Jay)
Notes:
Track 6 on K.I.D.S. 
Samples "Half Mast" by Empire of the Sun
Mac takes the beat and chorus from Empire of the Sun’s “Half Mast” in order to make a dope ass song

Although the lyrical content isn’t all that amazing, the real purpose for this song was to showcase his flow: how easily he can speed it up, break it up, and slow it down

“The Spins” is a term used to describe the spinning sensation felt by some people when intoxicated by weed and alcohol simultaneously.

Name:
Traffic In The Sky
(prod. Tecknowledgy)
Notes:
Track 7 on K.I.D.S.
Samples "Gymnopedie?" by 岩崎琢 (Taku Iwasaki)
“Traffic in the Sky”, one of the more laid back tracks from the original release of K.I.D.S., has been referred to as a “precursor to a lot of [Mac’s] older music”. However, when K.I.D.S. was re-released in 2020 and brought to streaming platforms, “Traffic in the Sky” was notably missing; the instrumental, produced by Tecknowledgy, is built around a sample of “Gymnopedie?”—a track from the 2003 anime Get Backers—which is the only sample they were unable to clear.

Interestingly enough, this song could be named after Jack Johnson’s track with the same title. Jack Johnson was one of Mac Miller’s favorite artists in middle school.

Name:
Knock Knock
(prod. ID Labs)
Notes:
Track 12 on K.I.D.S.
Samples "I've Told Every Little Star" by Linda Scott
With an earworm sample from Linda Scott’s “I’ve Told Every Little Star,” the lead single from Mac Miller’s mixtape K.I.D.S. peaked at #88 on the Hot 100 after getting a boost in popularity when HBO’s 24/7 Flyers/Rangers: Road to the NHL Winter Classic revealed that the Flyers used it as a victory song.

Music video director Rex Arrow provided some background information on “Knock Knock”:

When Malcolm first played me the track, we both kind of jumped to ‘It would be cool to do something that was kind of a throwback.’ That sort of slowly evolved, and the idea of mixing black and white, and color came in. I had done some dance stuff in college, and musical theatre in high school. I was like, ‘Look, man, it may sound strange but I think it would be cool if we kinda did something that felt a little Westside Story.’ It was a cold day in November. We shot the whole thing in one day, which is crazy to think about now.

Name:
Mad Flava, Heavy Flow (Interlude)
(feat. DJ Bonic) (prod. DJ Bonics)
Notes:
Track 13 on K.I.D.S.
Samples "Wake Up (Reprise In The Sunshine)" by Brand Nubian, as well as "Listen Up" by Erule & "Everybody Loves the Sunshine" by Roy Ayers Ubiquity
The phrase “mad flavor heavy flow” is a quote from Casper to his friend Tully, from the movie KIDS. Tully and Casper are in Tully’s mom’s room stealing change, Casper is drinking juice, he finds a tampon and starts playing with it. He drops it in his drink like a teabag, and proceeds to ask Tully “Hey, have you ever pulled one of these out with your teeth? Oh, that’s right, your girls don’t bleed yet. Casper then proceeds to say, “Mine have mad flava, heavy flow” as he sucks the juice out of the tampon.

Name:
Kool Aid & Frozen Pizza
(prod. Lord Finesse)
Notes:
Track 14 on K.I.D.S.
Remixes "Hip 2 Da Game" by Lord Finesse
On “Kool Aid & Frozen Pizza,” Mac raps over Lord Finesse’s instrumental from his 1995 track “Hip 2 Da Game.” In 2012, Lord Finesse sued Mac for $10 million for using the instrumental. While Mac stated he was only paying homage, Lord Finesse replied:

Mixtapes are one thing, but you can’t take someone’s else’s entire song, shoot a music video and call it your own

The “Kool Aid & Frozen Pizza” music video played a key part in Mac’s initial surge in popularity (garnering 65 million views as of 2024). Mac and Finesse eventually settled outside of court.

(prod. ID Labs)
Notes:
Track 1 on Best Day Ever
Samples "Beauties Can Die" by M83
Mac Miller has the following to say about the track:

"It’s a real trippy vibe to it and it’s supposed to be an epic introduction to get you ready to listen to everything. It sums up the message of this project [Best Day Ever]. No matter what, just trying to capture a happy and positive vibe to a situation."

It was recorded on two different instrumentals, both of which were produced by ID Labs, that started and ended the mixtape Best Day Ever.

Name:
Donald Trump
(prod. SAP)
Notes:
Track 3 on Best Day Ever
Samples "Vesuvius" by Sufjan Stevens

Mac himself had this to say about the song:

"It’s fun. I’m not saying this huge, crazy message in it, but the message is kind of deeper than what I say. It’s still a party, but underneath all that, it’s letting you know that you’re about to do something big."

Producer Sap said the following when asked about his favorite Mac Miller songs:

"Of course, “Donald Trump,” because we just met each other and that was insane. He killed it. I love when Mac used to go in on shit, rather than just crafting a song. Nobody expected [“Donald Trump”] to blow up and do that. You can’t really predict that. And we made that record just for fun, we just made something."

5 years after the release of this 2011 single, Mac made it clear that he in no way supported Donald Trump or his presidential ambitions. He took several shots at Trump on The Nightly Show, exclaiming “I fucking hate you!”

Donald Trump himself has tweeted about this song a few times

So we would always mess with each other. So it was like he was annoyed with me [laughs]; I did something that made him say “Oy vey.” He came with that sample, too. It came together from there."

Name:
Love Me as I Have Loved You 
(prod. Ritz Reynolds)
Notes:
“Love Me As I Have Loved You” serves as the intro to Mac Miller’s introspective and psychedelic mixtape Macadelic. The track features no vocals from Mac; instead, there are assorted vocals from women of different ethnicities that were at his house at the time. As Mac was having them record vocals to use throughout the mixtape, one of the women—an unnamed French woman—asked him if she could recite a poem that she had written about her first experience with love. Mac said he thought this was perfect, as the theme of Macadelic is exploring the relationship between love and drugs.

The title “Love Me As I Have Loved You” may be a reference to John 13:34 from the Bible:

A new command I give you: Love one another—as I have loved you, so you must love one another

Name:
Desperado 
(prod. ID Labs)
Notes:
Samples Gene Wilder in the 1971 film Willy Wonka & the Chocolate Factory, as well as (I’m Not Ready to) Settle Down by Little Ben & The Cheers, While My Guitar Gently Weeps by Jimmy Ponder, and Lucy in the Sky With Diamonds by The Beatles

“Desperado” touches on Mac’s struggles with mental health, fame and the cutthroat music industry. He talks about feeling isolated and alone despite the fame and money he has earned. Mac also mentions the drug use and sleep deprivation that come with his lifestyle. “Desperado” is a personal song for Mac, with content that reflects his own experiences as a successful musician. Despite his fame and money, he admits that the lifestyle comes with many drawbacks and challenges.

Name:

(prod. ID Labs)
Notes:
Samples Lucy in the Sky with Diamonds by The Beatles

Loud is the third song and the lead single from Mac Miller’s 2012 mixtape Macadelic.

Mac Miller makes this party hit, rapping about his experience with drugs, girls and money. He continues Verse 1 by rapping about his success as having the number 1 spot on Billboard and doing a lot of shows.

This track did chart at number 1 on Billboard’s Heatseekers and was certified gold by RIAA.

Name:

(prod. Sap)
Notes:
Samples Make Me Love You by Pivot

“Thoughts From A Balcony” is an introspective track describing Mac’s observations from the “top of the world” or the top of the rap game.

He also imagines the world without him, and how those close to him would react if he was gone. The track is overall one of the more inward-looking cuts from Macadelic.

He knows she (the drug) could never love him back, meanwhile he has devoted his life and fell victim to addiction. Hints suggest his compulsive dependence on promethazine with codeine, but more accurately, cocaine, if not both.

He criticizes the thoughts of society, common sense and human stupidity, wondering if anyone will remember him for all of his good deeds instead of for his ‘mistakes’, for not being an average person (regarding education, spending money, having sex with random women and consuming several drugs). He does an introspection, but contradicts himself a lot. He is his own worst enemy.

Name:
The Question 
(feat. Lil Wayne) (prod. Wally West & ID Labs)
Notes:
Mac teams up with Lil Wayne to “question” life and who they really have become.
Mac can’t seem to find an answer to who he really is and what he should do with his life so he just turns to drugs and keeps doing what he does.

During the time of making Macadellic, Mac Miller had released Blue Slide Park which was not received well by critics. Also during this time he became addicted to lean and gained a lot of weight because of its use.

This song or these questions that Mac is asking himself throughout song are the kind of questions that we ask ourselves when we are going through a particularly rough time as the one Mac was going through at the time.

The song is about that question, “what purpose do we serve in this life, what part do we play in this grand universe of life?”

Its a very existential question to ask yourself which can lead you through states of deep thoughts.

Mac brilliantly throughout the song has a verse within a verse. He uses different voices which could easily stand alone without each other but are perfectly in unison when put next to each other. In the beginning he poses question to himself and answers them, but he leans away from this and just gives a narrative in a different voice.

Name:
America 
(feat. Joey Bada$$ & Casey Veggies) (prod. Hannibal King)
Notes:
Samples Danger! She's A Stranger by Five Stairsteps

“America” marks the first and only official collaboration between Mac Miller and Pro Era star Joey Bada$$. However, on April 25, 2012, two months after “America”’s release, Joey and Pro Era opened a show for him after Mac reached out to their manager. Joey described this experience to Rolling Stone:

"I been listening to Mac since I was 16, 15. A lot of people don’t know this, but Mac actually gave me one of my first big opportunities, one of my first big shots in the game — I remember I was 17, and I can’t forget the date, April 25, 2012. Before my first mixtape came out and everything. Mac Miller reached out to my manager and he asked for us to open up. It’s so funny, because it was a school night! We were still in high school at the time. I don’t think a lot of people understood that. We literally left school that day and we went to this Mac Miller show to open up for him for 5,000 kids. I just remember all of us, we felt like we could take over the world after that."

Mac Miller and producer Hannibal King have been collaborating as early as 2009, when Mac and Beedie (collectively known as The Ill Spoken at the time) appeared on “Rock It On” from Hannibal’s mixtape Eating Cornbread on the Millennium Falcon. On March 3, 2011, XXL Magazine released 2011’s Freshman Class: The Mixtape which included “Bruce Wayne”. A year after the release of “America”, Mac’s team Most Dope released “CPR”.

Name:
Life Can Wait 
(prod. Larry Lovestein, Bob “Grumpy” Loveskins, Pierce “Gutter Ball” Fortenauté & Ralph Bubbletea)
Notes:
The introduction to Larry Lovestein’s debut album You. Larry Lovestein, aka Mac Miller, uses low and high-pitched vocals at the beginning of the track to introduce himself as this new jazzy, soul-influenced, Jewish singer with an Afro, moniker. Mac has been known to make projects under alter egos like Larry Fisherman for instrumental productions, Larry Lovestein for jazz, Delusional Thomas for horror-core rap, and smaller titles like Larry Dollaz and Smoke-Mo' Purp. Malcolm continues to widen his range of music with this incredible all original jazz album.

“Life Can Wait” is the intro to the entire project. Larry is so deeply in love with this girl that he’s really saying life can wait. He can forget about his entire life for the moment and have nothing but this girl on his focus. Love is powerful. “Love brought us here today.” Larry just wants all of her, she’s just simply amazing.

Name:
Baby It's Cold Outside 
(with Ariana Grande)
Notes:
Interpolates Baby, It's Cold Outside by Johnny Mercer & Margaret Whiting


This rendition of “Baby, It’s Cold Outside” sees Larry Lovestein—Mac Miller’s jazz persona—and Ariana Grande coming together for a special Christmas collaboration. Although Lovestein is usually credited alongside his band The Velvet Revival, he makes a rare solo appearance here. The cover was uploaded to YouTube by Ariana on Christmas of 2012, but was later taken down.

Name:
Birthday 
(prod. Larry Fisherman)
Notes:
Uses samples from the 1974 film The Great Gatsby based on F. Scott Fitzgerald’s 1925 novel of the same name.
This film is sampled many times throughout the mixtape.

"The day before we left, we have had a great conversation with Dalai Lama. He talked about ways to avoid wars, how to save the environment and the world population, how to avoid global catastrophes that our civilization will face. He talked about the simplest things, and it seemed that in his words, there wasn’t anything new for us. But this conversation made us look at the world in a different way.

The dialogue is sampled from a 2008 Russian documentary titled Рассвет/Закат. Далай-Лама 14 (Rassvet/Zakat. Dalai Lama 14 when romanized, Sunrise/Sunset: Dalai Lama 14 in English). The film is directed and narrated by Vitaly Mansky, and revolves around the history and the daily life of the 14th Dalai Lama, Tenzin Gyatso."

In the outro to “Birthday”, we hear the Dalai Lama speaking about the concepts of relativity and absolutism, which are often explored in Buddhism.

The dialogue is sampled from a 2008 Russian documentary titled Рассвет/Закат. Далай-Лама 14 (Rassvet/Zakat. Dalai Lama 14 transliterated, Sunrise/Sunset: Dalai Lama 14 in English). Era: Watching Movies With The Sound Off
Name:
The Star Room
(prod. randomblackdude)
Notes:
Introducing his second studio album with an introspective track produced by Earl Sweatshirt (who produced a good portion of the tracks featured on Watching Movies with The Sound Off), Mac Miller directly addresses his issues with hedonism and his perception of a higher power.

Paradoxical whilst addressing his inability to commit to action, “The Star Room” sets the tone for the rest of the album. Marking the introduction of Mac Miller’s horrorcore alter-ego Delusional Thomas, this single articulated a level of internal conflict beyond that of anything featured within Macadelic.

Less than a year after Mac’s passing, his former partner-in-rhyme Beedie shed some light on the origin of the title:

"The ‘Star Room’ was a room in my mom’s old house in Point Breeze, and he made the song about it. That was my bedroom at one point, and then it was our studio. That’s where he made some of his first songs, and up there he decided This is what I’m doing with my life. He shouted it out on the song. We had glow in the dark stickers of stars on the ceiling which were on the ceiling when my mom moved into the house in 2003. We didn’t put them up there, but we didn’t take them down. It was in the attic. At one point, I slept up there, and then, later on, it was a studio."

Words from Mac Miller about The Star Room: "I just wanted to start the album off really honestly you know and so like the two voices there's a voice and that's kind of like like a subconscious or voice in my head right and it's like a different character that's starting the album you know if we're like in a circus type event right there's person that comes out first needs really weird and creepy and he's like are you everywhere and everyone's kind of like what the fuck going on who the fuck is this little dude then the curtain opens in this in the show starts you know that's kind of that guy and and then that's why the first starts you know but me I'm still because it's like boom and then see yourself and then same shit and then yeah"

Excerpt from the Watching Movies with the Sound Off essay: "Represents the beginning of Life, The Birth, Innocence and pure question. It represents birth but not necessarily meaning ignorance. It’s birth with not complete understanding of whats going on in the world, but a smarter approach on learning."

“Avian” derives its name from Mac’s love of nature and wildlife documentaries, Birds of the Gods in particular. Mac has described this song as “the heart and soul” of this album in an interview with Billboard.

Produced by Mac under the alias of Larry Fisherman, the song features a sped up sample from the soundtrack to the 2009 Disney movie Up—an apt choice for the song’s imagery.

Words from Mac Miller about Avian: "I love this song this song is like the heart and soul, produced by me then the hook is my favorite part of the song cuz this hook is very simple then after you like put down your like preconceived like oh that's weird that's weird then you think you like hey"

Excerpt from the Watching Movies with the Sound Off essay: "The First Time flying. It represents the feeling of freedom. Free thought. I wonder how it must feel to fly but not be born into the ability of flying, its as if you are learning to eat for the first time and never did before, “ooohhhh this is living” Flying just seems so majestic, and so graceful, it’s one of the only things we will never be able to experience. do those birds even know how good they have it?"

Excerpt from the Watching Movies with the Sound Off essay: "after all this freedom and bliss, you realize you are on a different level of comprehension then ever before and going back to regular life is less satisfying. You feel as if everything in your head is reality and everything else isn’t. but what you have been told all your life leads you to believe that you are not real. (mention of love: she doesn’t understand you either. It surprises you that she of all people, your “best friend” your “partner” can’t make the trip with you)"

(prod. Flying Lotus)
Notes:
04 SDS (mastered).mp3
Samples Ryō Horikawa, Masako Nozawa, and Iemasa Kayumi in the 1993 film Dragon Ball Z: Broly the Legendary Super Saiyan

The track S.D.S. is an abbreviation for “Somebody Do Something.” “Somebody Do Something” was a running joke between Miller and his friends
Producer Flying Lotus seemed to have incorporated a sample from the movie ‘Dragon Ball Z: Broly the Legendary Super Saiyan’. The sample features protagonist Goku telling Broly that his name isn’t Kakarot, but Son Goku.

It may sound gibberish to non-Japanese listeners since the sample is from the Japanese movie, and not a Western release.

 Words from Mac Miller about S.D.S.: "I was so nervous to spit that verse that I didn't think that it was gonna be the one that that we use because I was kind of like here we go I guess its fucking Flying Lotus you better fucking kill it and I remember like I rap on it and he's like hey my I didn't expect you to go in like that"

Excerpt from the Watching Movies with the Sound Off essay: "Oh Shit, “reality” is kicking in. Somebody Do Somethin! what do i do? it’s panic in realizing maybe you aren’t so certain about what is going around you. Now you are kind of playing with what could be fact and what could be fiction."

This song on Watching Movies with the Sound Off brings the concept into light: proposing ideas. Mac mentions a few things that people criticize him for, or what even he criticizes himself for. The first few lines were featured in an episode of “Mac Miller and The Most Dope Family.”

Words from Mac Miller about I Am Who Am (Killin' Time): "Im laying in the studio, I'm sleeping on the beanbag which at this point I've been using these beanbag so much they're not even beanbags at this point they're just like flat on the ground. So I'm sleeping his beanbag and Q walks into the studio and the doors open the sunlight and I'm like uh like I wake up to him going through beats like that was my last thought when I went to sleep and I woke up in this beats playing and I'm like yo I was like this is fucking crazy. I literally woke right up I was like you like this is it, loaded up wrote that verse. It wasn't a lot of thought it like they just happened."

Excerpt from the Watching Movies with the Sound Off essay: "wait a second... you were onto something. don't quit.
Why did u let the stress get to you. You had it all figured out. It was all about not having shit figured out and searching. Did you ever feel that you used your intelligence as much as you did when you were living as a part time philosopher taking a break from producing something for people to listen to but just producing ideas about the things unknown. what is more important than an idea? that is where anything substantial comes from..it all grows from an idea. that was working. let's get back to that place.

(Love Interlude) - let's go talk to the old heads man. We were just feeling so good about being the old heads maybe we forgot about OUR old heads. actually, fuck OUR old heads. let's talk to their old head's old head. The first thing these old married motherfuckers wanna talk about is love. "That's what it's all about" they say. I was running around just like you, goin wild, living free until i realized that i should just stop disrespecting these women, find the one and settle down and start reproducing and building the future, creating life. hmmm… maybe ill try that."

Here you have a sad girl that you just can't make happy anymore. You keep trying to tell her, Let's go! we can leave this place. we can leave reality, we don't have to be here, leave it all behind, let's make our own world. Let's define what life is. Let's make it up. I know your life is hard, i see your pain, but you don't have to live inside your pain anymore. we can be free. Just as these birds can decide their own path. let's fly, let's just be free. (she declines) (she just can't trust you, she can't trust your ideas" fuck it. why did that old head tell me that shit anyways, i would have been better off where i was before."

Era: Watching Movies With The Sound Off
Name:
Red Dot Music
(feat. Action Bronson) (prod. The Alchemist)
Notes:
Samples Attention by Head West, as well as A Heart's Desire by Camel

On “Red Dot Music”, Mac Miller raps about his drug experiences. Some experiences so extreme that he felt like almost overdosing. He also questions his belief in God, humankind, and the concept of God while being on extreme doses of drugs.

The title “Red Dot Music” references the beam from a laser attachment for a gun, except the gun is the criticism and hate that he has been getting. The title of the song may also be a reference to fellow Pittsburgh rapper and Taylor Gang affiliate Chevy Woods' 2011 mixtape Red Cup Music. Mac collaborated with Woods on his debut major label mixtape K.I.D.S. on the track Paper Route and co-produced the track Fucked Up from Red Cup Music with Big Jerm.

Words from Mac Miller on Red Dot Music: "I love how that song follows objects like after such like a beautiful song and then just comes in like really nicely my favorite part is like the hook "I keep watch till I'm dead""

Exceprt from the Watching Movies with the Sound Off essay: "Now you been thru some shit. Real Ups and Downs in ways you couldn't have imagined before. Fuck this shit. You approaching life with a new view, fuck everybody, they aint shit. What makes these dudes as tight as you anyways? Don't trust anybody, they all just comin for your spot anyways. No One is your friend they want you to fail, they all praying on your downfall. They got the guns aimed at you, waiting for u to give them a reason to pull the trigger, How are you going to act now that everybody doubting unsupportive eyes are on you. They are watching...what do you do?"

(feat. ScHoolboy Q) (prod. Chuck Inglish)
Notes:
On “Gees,” Mac flexes his ease in dominating the rap game and his skills with the ladies, while his alter-ego, Delusional Thomas, introduces his selfish personality, such as not including anybody in his will and misogynistic tendencies. Q, on the other hand, talks briefly of his rags-to-riches story, his blazing, and doing some pretty freaky stuff with a nun. An overall satirical and fun track that serves as a jovial detour from the otherwise depressive sound and subject matter on the rest of the album.

Words from Mac Miller about Gees: "I was like oh I got the hook and Chuck looks and then I was like just said suck my dick before I slap it with it and I was like that's it and chuck was kind of like like he was like really like that's the hook I was like yeah I was like that's what else needs to be said he's like like the hit maker Chuck Inglish so like goes in there and listen to these like this might be crazy enough to work like yeah"

Excerpt from the Watching Movies with the Sound Off essay: "all eyes are on you, you better not come thru on your soft "i wanna change the world, and am very philo-sophical shit, that'll make you look like a bitch. It would probably be better to just act crazy saying whatever outrageous things come to your mind whenever you want.
Although it's not as idiotic as it seems. You are actually excercising your mind in different forms of creativity and using stupidity as a display and form of intelligence. clever you are."

Era: Watching Movies With The Sound Off
Name:
Watching Movies
(prod. Sap & Larry Fisherman)
Notes:
Samples You Are Dead by The End of Science

Watching Movies was the second single made public prior to the release of Mac Miller’s sophomore album, Watching Movies with the Sound Off.

Exercising a level of self-aware narcissism that Mac had already parodied prior to its release, the eleventh track on this LP epitomised such arrogance in a manner not so tongue in cheek, contrasting divinity with the materialistic cliches exhibited by many other successful rappers in the music video that later accompanied it.

This song is dedicated to Mac’s deceased friend Reuben Eli Mitrani (whose initials are capitalized in the title). Mac and Reuben had been good friends since 2002 before he passed in September 2012. Not only did Mac dedicate this song to Reuben, he also named his independent record label REMember Music.

The album version, however, is at a much faster tempo and doesn’t have the chopped and screwed feel. Mac stated in an interview that this song embodies juxtaposed messages.
